experiences.intentions:
Player 2: No concrete role info yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As Good Servant on round 1, approving an initial 2-person team is generally fine to gather information and avoid unnecessary early rejects; team [0,3] contains the leader and one other with no evidence against them. My vote reveals a cooperative opening stance and doesn't significantly harm Good.
Player 1: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns; only that player 3 as leader proposed [3,0], which is a standard opening 2-person team with limited info. As a Good Servant in round 1, approving a reasonable first team is usually optimal to generate quest information and avoid wasting early votes. My vote reveals little beyond being open to an information-gathering first quest, which does not significantly hurt Good.
Player 3: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0,1,2 are unknown good roles. The current phase is team voting on quest 1 for team [0,3], which I proposed. As evil Assassin on a 2-person first quest, having myself on the team gives evil a chance to fail immediately or build ambiguity later. Approving my own proposed team is optimal and consistent. My vote reveals little since leaders usually approve their own team, and rejecting would look suspicious and reduce evil control.
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,3] includes a known evil, so as Good I should reject to avoid risking an immediate failed quest and to signal concern without exposing too much. Rejecting helps Good by pushing for a cleaner team next round.
Player 4: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team includes one evil. On quest 1 with 2 players, sending 3 with an unknown good player gives evil a chance to fail immediately. As evil, approving a team with my teammate on it is strong; early approvals are not especially suspicious, and rejecting could unnecessarily block an advantageous mission. My vote reveals mild support for a team containing 3, but on round 1 that is not too informative and helps evil more than it hurts.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are Evil. Proposed team [0,1,4] includes known Evil 4, so from Good perspective this team is bad and should be rejected. Voting reject also avoids giving Evil a chance to fail quest 2 and reveals little beyond standard suspicion of 4, which is acceptable.
Player 1: Quest 1 team [3,0] succeeded, so 0 and 3 each look somewhat good, though one could still be evil. Proposed team [0,1,4] keeps 0 from the successful quest and includes me, which is good from my perspective, but swaps out 3 for untested 4. As a good servant, early game I should generally approve reasonable teams that include a previously successful member and myself, especially on round 1 where information is limited. Approving reveals little beyond willingness to test 4 while preserving some continuity from a success, which helps gather info without excessive rejection risk.
Player 2: Quest 1 succeeded with team [3,0], so 0 and 3 look somewhat better, though a single success doesn't fully clear either. Proposed team [0,1,4] includes only one member from the known-success team and adds two unknowns, making it riskier than necessary. As a Good servant in team voting, I should favor teams with stronger evidence; rejecting preserves information and pressures leaders to include more cleared players. My reject vote signals preference for evidence-based teams without giving Evil much useful info, since Good should generally resist broad unknown teams early.
Player 3: I know player 4 is evil with me; players 0 and 1 are unknown but team [0,1,4] contains one guaranteed evil, so if approved we get an evil on quest 2. Last quest [3,0] succeeded with 0 fails; since I was on it, I passed to avoid outing myself, so 0 is still ambiguous to others. In team voting as evil, the optimal play is usually to approve teams containing evil and reject all-good teams. Approving here is strong for evil because it gives us influence on the quest, and my vote itself reveals little since many good players may also approve early teams.
Player 4: I know Player 3 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 succeeded on team [3,0], which means 3 likely chose pass to avoid early exposure, so 0 remains ambiguous/good-leaning publicly. Current phase is team voting on my proposed team [0,1,4]. As evil, having myself on the team gives us sabotage potential, but approving also risks creating a public link if this team fails. Still, Quest 2 is a good opportunity to put evil on the board, and rejecting my own team is suspicious. Approving reveals little beyond consistency with my proposal and helps evil by getting an evil player onto the quest.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil. Team [0,1] excludes known evil players and is the strongest available 2-player team. Previous fail on [0,1,4] implicates 4, which I already know is evil, and 1 remains plausible good. Approving helps good and does not reveal extra info beyond supporting a clean team.
Player 1: Q1 team [3,0] succeeded, so 3 and 0 look somewhat better. Q2 team [0,1,4] had exactly one fail; since I am good, either 0 or 4 is evil. That makes this proposed team [0,1] risky with at least a 50% chance of containing evil, while excluding player 3 who is currently the strongest good signal. As a Servant on team voting, optimal play is to reject suspicious teams and preserve information/avoid handing evil an easy point. Rejecting also signals that I do not trust 0 yet after the failed quest.
Player 2: I know 0 was on both prior successful/failing teams, while 1 was only on the failed team with 4, so at least one of 1/4 is evil and 0 is somewhat more likely good from the first quest success. As a good Servant in team voting, I should reject teams that are too risky when better information can be gained later; this 2-person team [0,1] includes the main suspect 1 and excludes me, so approving risks handing evil another easy fail. My vote reveals skepticism of 1, which helps good coordinate around the strongest available inference from quest 2.
Player 4: I know player 3 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team [3,0] succeeded, which is consistent with either 0 being good or 3 choosing to hide. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] failed with exactly one fail, and since I was on it and played fail, that gives cover to 0 and 1 but does not clear them. Now the proposed quest 3 team is [0,1], which excludes both known evils 3 and 4. As evil in the voting phase, the optimal play is to reject a team that contains no evil players, since it is likely to succeed and put good at match point. My vote reveals some suspicion of [0,1], but that is acceptable because stopping an all-good team helps evil more than blending in here.
Player 3: I know Player 4 is evil with me. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] failed with exactly one fail, which strongly implicates 4 or is a useful frame on 0/1; since I know 4 is evil, this proposed team [0,1] excludes both evils and is likely all-good. In team voting as evil, optimal play is usually to reject good teams that have a strong chance to succeed, especially at 1-1 on Quest 3. My reject also aligns with skepticism about a 2-player team after a fail involving 0 and 1 on the previous quest, so it does not reveal much beyond normal caution. Rejecting helps evil by trying to block a likely successful quest without giving away extra info.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know 3 and 4 are Evil, so this proposed team [0,3] definitely contains an evil player. We are in team voting on quest 3, and as Merlin I should prevent evil-inclusive teams from going whenever possible. Rejecting reveals little beyond normal suspicion of 3, but approving would risk another fail and help Evil. The earlier success with [0,3] could have been 3 choosing to pass to gain cover, so that does not clear them.
Player 2: Player 3 and 0 were on the successful first quest, so both look relatively good. The failed second quest was [0,1,4], meaning exactly one of 1 or 4 is evil if 0 is good, which is likely given quest 1. Proposed team [0,3] matches the known successful pair and avoids the suspicious 1/4 pair. As Good, approving the most trustworthy team is optimal and reveals consistency with the evidence.
Player 3: I am evil Assassin with teammate 4. Quest 1 [0,3] succeeded, which mildly clears 0 and me publicly. Quest 2 [0,1,4] failed with exactly one fail, which strongly implicates 4 from my perspective but publicly could implicate 0,1,or4. Now proposed team [0,3] excludes my teammate and includes me. As evil on a 2-player quest, if approved I can fail and put evil at 2-1, while my earlier success gives me cover. Voting approve is optimal because the team contains an evil player (me) and success/failure control stays with us. My vote reveals little since many players may approve a previously successful pair.
Player 1: I’m Good Servant. Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 look relatively good. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] failed with exactly one fail; since I am good, either 0 or 4 is evil. Combining with Quest 1 success, 0 is less likely evil, making 4 the best evil candidate. Proposed team [0,3] repeats the only known successful pair and excludes 4, which is optimal for Good. Approving reveals consistency with evidence-based play and helps Good secure quest 3.
Player 4: I know 3 is my evil teammate and 4 is me, so team [0,3] contains exactly one known evil. Quest 1 with [3,0] succeeded, which may make 3 look good; quest 2 with [0,1,4] failed because I failed it, which implicates one of 0/1/4 from others' perspective. In team voting as evil, I want teams that include an evil player and preferably avoid exposing both evils together. [0,3] is strong for evil because 3 can choose to fail or pass strategically, and approving aligns with 3's established cover from the first quest. My approve slightly supports getting an evil onto the quest without directly outing additional information.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil. Proposed team [1,2,4] includes known evil 4, so it is extremely likely to fail and should be rejected. As good, I want to avoid sending a known evil on quest 4 when evil already has 2 fails. Rejecting also does not reveal much beyond reasonable suspicion of 4, which is acceptable.
Player 2: Two failed quests with exactly 1 fail each give limited info, but [0,1,4] had a fail and [0,3] had a fail, so 0 is highly suspicious by intersection. Team [1,2,4] excludes 0 and 3, which is plausibly better for Good. As a Servant on quest 4 at 1-2, we need the best available chance to avoid immediate loss; approving a team that cuts the strongest suspect is optimal. My vote reveals some trust in excluding 0; that is acceptable and helps coordinate Good.
Player 3: I know 4 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [1,2,4] includes exactly one evil I know. With evil already at 2 quest losses, approving a team with 4 on it gives us a strong chance to win immediately by failing quest 4. As evil, optimal play is to get this team through. My approve vote is consistent with wanting a winnable team; it reveals little because players often approve mixed-information teams on round 1.
Player 4: I know player 3 is evil with me, and this proposed team [1,2,4] contains only one known evil: me. Previous failed quests were [0,1,4] and [0,3], each with exactly one fail, which keeps suspicion spread across 0,1,3,4. As evil on quest 4 with evil already at 2 points, the optimal play is to get this team approved because I am on it and can fail the quest to win immediately. Approving reveals little beyond my willingness to back a team containing myself, and if it passes I can secure victory for evil.
Player 1: Known info: Q1 [0,1,4] had 1 fail, so at least one of 0/1/4 is evil; since I am good, at least one of 0 or 4 is evil. Q2 [0,3] was rejected heavily, giving limited info. Q3 [0,3] then failed with 1 fail, so at least one of 0 or 3 is evil. Combining with Q1 strongly points to 0 being evil; 4 could still be evil, 3 likely good if 0 is the saboteur there. Proposed team [1,2,4] excludes the strongest evil suspect 0, which is good, but includes 4 who is suspicious from Q1 and 2 who proposed this team with little clearing info. At 1 good / 2 evil, good must be conservative and reject teams containing significant unresolved suspicion when there are better alternatives like [1,2,3] or [1,3,4] depending reads. My vote reveals skepticism toward 4 and caution under must-win pressure, which helps good more than approving a risky team.
